front: What does SEC registration of a security mean — and NOT mean?
back: The issuer met full and fair disclosure requirements. It is NOT approval, a guarantee of accuracy, or a judgment on merit.
$500,000 per separate customer, of which no more than $250,000 may be cash. Covers broker-dealer failure — never market losses.
$250,000 per depositor, per insured bank, per ownership category. Bank deposits only — never securities, even those sold at a bank.

front: Who enforces MSRB rules at broker-dealers?
back: FINRA. The MSRB writes rules but has no enforcement arm — and it never regulates municipal issuers themselves.
Broker = agent, earns a commission. Dealer = principal trading its own inventory, earns a markup/markdown. One capacity per trade.
None directly — only courts can. The SEC brings civil actions and refers criminal cases to the DOJ. FINRA can censure, fine, suspend, expel, or bar.
Transfer agent RECORDS ownership changes and replaces certificates. Registrar COUNTS shares so outstanding never exceeds authorized.
Advice about securities, as a Business, for Compensation — all three means registration under the Advisers Act of 1940 and fiduciary duty.
State securities laws (Uniform Securities Act framework) requiring state-level registration of securities, broker-dealers, and agents.
Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation — centralized clearing, settlement, and book-entry custody for the securities markets.
Primary: new securities, proceeds to the ISSUER (1933 Act). Secondary: investors trade outstanding shares with each other (1934 Act).
OTC trading of exchange-LISTED securities.
Institutions trading directly with each other with no broker-dealer intermediary.
An ATS with no pre-trade quote display; trades still print to the tape after execution. Used to work institutional blocks without market impact.

front: Customers buy at the ___ and sell at the ___
back: Buy at the ASK (offer); sell at the BID. The spread is the dealer's compensation.
National Best Bid and Offer — highest bid and lowest ask across all venues. Reg NMS forbids trading through better protected quotes.

front: Monetary vs fiscal policy — who does what?
back: Monetary: the Federal Reserve (money supply, rates). Fiscal: Congress and the President (taxing and spending).

front: Fed BUYS securities in open market operations →
back: Money supply rises, interest rates fall — easing. Fed SELLS = tightening (rates rise).

front: Which rate does the Fed set directly?
back: The discount rate (Fed lending to banks). The fed funds rate (bank-to-bank overnight) is a market rate the FOMC only targets.
Expansion → Peak → Contraction → Trough → (repeat). Recession ≈ 2+ quarters of declining GDP.
Stock prices (S&P 500), building permits, new unemployment claims (also: new orders, consumer expectations). They move BEFORE the economy.
Short-term yields above long-term — the classic bond-market recession warning.
U.S. EXPORTS (American goods cheaper abroad). A strong dollar helps imports and U.S. tourists abroad.
Stagnant growth and high unemployment WITH high inflation.
Utilities, food, pharmaceuticals, tobacco — steady demand in any economy. Cyclical: autos, airlines, homebuilders, luxury.

front: What may be sent during the cooling-off period?
back: Preliminary prospectus (red herring), tombstone ads, and indications of interest. NO sales, NO accepting funds, NO research reports.

front: Is an indication of interest binding?
back: No — binding on neither the customer nor the underwriter.
Unlimited accredited investors + up to 35 non-accredited but sophisticated purchasers; no general solicitation. 506(c): advertising OK but ALL buyers accredited.
Income $200k ($300k joint) in each of the last 2 years, or $1M+ net worth EXCLUDING the primary residence.
Per 90 days: the GREATER of 1% of shares outstanding or the average weekly volume of the prior 4 weeks.

front: Rule 144 holding period (restricted stock, reporting issuer)
back: 6 months for non-affiliates, then freely salable. Control stock has no holding period but always faces volume limits.
Firm commitment: underwriter buys the whole issue as PRINCIPAL — underwriter bears risk. Best efforts: underwriter is AGENT — issuer bears risk.
Register once, sell portions over up to 3 years as conditions allow.

front: Largest piece of the underwriting spread
back: The selling concession — paid to whoever actually sells the shares.
U.S. government and municipal securities, bank securities, and commercial paper with maturity ≤ 270 days.
Issued − treasury. Treasury stock has no vote and receives no dividends.
No — the board declares dividends. Shareholders vote on directors, splits, and new share issuance.
SMALL shareholders — all votes can be concentrated on one board seat.
Skipped dividends accrue and must be paid in full before common gets any dividend.
Rights: short-term, to existing shareholders, priced BELOW market. Warrants: long-term sweeteners, exercise price ABOVE market at issuance.

front: Main risk an ADR investor keeps
back: Currency (exchange-rate) risk — dollar-denominated, but value and dividends track the foreign currency.
DERP: Declaration, Ex-date, Record, Payable — and the EX-DATE IS THE SAME DAY as the record date. Buy before the ex-date to get the dividend.

front: Are stock splits and stock dividends taxable when received?
back: No — total value is unchanged; the per-share cost basis adjusts instead.
Secured creditors → general creditors/debentures → subordinated debentures → preferred → common (always last).
Par ÷ conversion price. A $1,000 bond convertible at $40 = 25 shares.
Inverse. Rates up → prices down. Most volatile: long maturity + low coupon (worst case: long-term zeros).

front: Yield order for a DISCOUNT bond
back: Coupon < current yield < YTM < YTC. (Reverse the whole chain for a premium bond.)
Annual interest ÷ current market price. A 6% bond at $800: 60 ÷ 800 = 7.5%.
4–52 week maturities, no coupon — sold at a discount, quoted on a discount-yield basis. Zero default risk.
Federally taxable; EXEMPT from state and local tax. (Municipal interest is the mirror image.)

front: Only agency backed by full faith and credit of the U.S.
back: GNMA (Ginnie Mae). FNMA and FHLMC are government-sponsored, not guaranteed.
Prepayment risk — homeowners refinance when rates fall, returning principal at the worst time to reinvest.
GO: taxing power, usually needs voter approval. Revenue: earnings of a specific project (tolls, fees) — no vote, analyzed by feasibility/coverage.
Muni yield ÷ (1 − tax bracket). 4% muni at 32% bracket ⇒ 4 ÷ 0.68 = 5.88%.
BBB− (S&P/Fitch) / Baa3 (Moody's). Anything below = high yield ('junk').
When rates FALL (refinance cheaply). Holders exercise PUT features when rates RISE.
Corporates & munis: 30/360. Government notes/bonds: actual/365. Accrues up to but NOT including settlement.
Zero-coupon bonds, income (adjustment) bonds, and bonds in default — no accrued interest.

front: A corporate bond point and an eighth are worth…
back: 1 point = $10; ⅛ point = $1.25. Governments trade in 32nds ($0.3125 each).
8.5% of the POP — and only if the fund offers breakpoints, rights of accumulation, and reinvestment at NAV.

front: POP formula from NAV and load
back: POP = NAV ÷ (1 − sales charge %). NAV $19.32, 5.5% load ⇒ $20.44.
Forward pricing — all orders execute at the NEXT computed NAV (calculated daily at market close). Redemption paid within 7 days.

front: Class A vs B vs C shares
back: A: front-end load with breakpoints — best for large, long-term. B: declining CDSC, converts to A. C: level load, no conversion — shorter horizons.
Selling just below a breakpoint to earn more commission — a violation. LOI (13 months, backdatable 90 days) and rights of accumulation reach breakpoints.
0.25% — 12b-1 fees pay DISTRIBUTION/marketing costs, not management.

front: Which fund can trade at a discount to NAV?
back: Closed-end funds — fixed shares trading on exchanges at supply/demand prices. Open-end funds always transact at NAV (+ load).
Fixed, UNMANAGED portfolio; self-liquidating; no board, no adviser; redeemable units.
90%+ of taxable income to shareholders → no corporate tax on it. Dividends taxed to holders as ORDINARY income. No loss pass-through.

front: Variable annuity: who bears investment risk?
back: The INVESTOR — separate-account value fluctuates. That's why it's a security (prospectus + insurance license). Fixed annuity: insurer bears risk, insurance only.
Actual separate-account return ABOVE the AIR → next payment rises; below → falls; equal → level.

front: Annuity payout with the largest monthly check
back: Straight life — payments stop at death, nothing to heirs. Joint & last survivor pays the least.
Earnings taxed as ORDINARY income (LIFO for non-qualified); 10% penalty on earnings before age 59½. Growth is tax-DEFERRED, not tax-free.

front: Long call: right, outlook, max gain/loss
back: Right to BUY at strike. Bullish. Max gain unlimited; max loss = premium. Breakeven = strike + premium.

front: Long put: right, outlook, max gain/loss
back: Right to SELL at strike. Bearish. Max gain = strike − premium (stock to zero); max loss = premium. Breakeven = strike − premium.

front: Which option position has unlimited risk?
back: The uncovered (naked) CALL writer — must buy at any market price to deliver at the strike.

front: When is a call in the money?
back: Market price ABOVE the strike (puts: below). Premium is irrelevant. Intrinsic value = the in-the-money amount.
Own the stock + write a call: income and a premium cushion, upside capped at the strike. Most conservative writing strategy.

front: Best hedge for a LONG stock position
back: BUY a put — a guaranteed floor at the strike. (Writing a call only cushions by the premium.)

front: Best hedge for a SHORT stock position
back: BUY a call — caps the repurchase price at the strike against unlimited upside risk.

front: Who issues and guarantees listed options?
back: The OCC (Options Clearing Corporation). Equity options are American-style; expiration is the third Friday of the month.
100 shares. A premium of $3 costs $300.

front: Maximum loss for any option BUYER
back: The premium paid — always. (Writer's max gain = the premium, always.)

front: 529 withdrawals for qualified education are taxed…
back: Federally TAX-FREE. Non-qualified: earnings taxed as ordinary income + 10% penalty. Donor keeps control; beneficiary changeable within family.
A donor may front-load 5 years of annual gift-tax exclusions in one contribution.

front: Unique tax feature of DPPs/limited partnerships
back: Flow-through of income AND losses (passive — only offset passive income). No corporate tax.

front: Limited partner who manages the business risks…
back: Losing limited liability — being treated as a general partner.

front: Biggest practical risk of a DPP interest
back: Illiquidity — no real secondary market.
'2 and 20' — 2% management fee + 20% performance fee. Accredited investors only; lock-up periods.

front: Core risk of structured products and ETNs
back: The issuing bank's CREDIT risk — they are unsecured debt. Not FDIC or SIPC protected; often illiquid with capped upside.
Systematic (market, interest-rate, inflation) hits everything — diversification does NOT help. Unsystematic (business, credit) — diversification is the cure.
The stock tends to move 50% more than the market in either direction. Beta measures market (systematic) risk.

front: Investments most exposed to inflation risk
back: Fixed-income and cash — fixed payments lose purchasing power. Defenses: equities, real assets, TIPS.
Rate risk hurts when rates RISE (prices fall). Reinvestment risk hurts when rates FALL (coupons/calls reinvest lower). Zeros: max rate risk, zero reinvestment risk.
Wide bid-ask spread / no ready buyer. High: DPPs, non-traded REITs, thin small-caps. Low: Treasuries, listed large-caps.

front: Employee with most of her 401(k) in employer stock has…
back: Concentration risk — savings and paycheck tied to one company. Fix: diversify.
Buy Limits and Sell Stops BELOW the market; sell limits and buy stops above.
Market: execution guaranteed, price not. Limit: price or better guaranteed, execution not.

front: What does a sell stop become when triggered?
back: A MARKET order (fills at the next price, maybe far below). A stop-LIMIT becomes a limit order — price protected but may never fill.

front: Regular-way settlement for stocks and corporate/muni bonds
back: T+1 (since May 2024). Treasuries and listed options: also T+1. Cash settlement: same day, both parties must agree.

front: Reg T payment deadline and the penalty for missing it
back: S+2 (trade date + 3 business days total). Unpaid: position sold out and the account FROZEN 90 days (cash up front).
Offer to buy shares directly at a premium, limited window, each holder chooses. You may tender only shares you are LONG.
Fewer shares at a higher price — typically to avoid delisting. Total value unchanged; requires shareholder approval.

front: FINRA 5% policy applies to… and NOT to…
back: Secondary-market commissions, markups, and markdowns (a GUIDE, not a rule). NOT to prospectus offerings like mutual funds and new issues.

front: Can a firm charge a markup AND a commission on one trade?
back: No — one capacity, one charge per trade. Capacity is disclosed on the confirmation.
Reasonable diligence to obtain the most favorable price available for the customer under the circumstances — required in every capacity.

front: Is a customer's signature required to open a cash account?
back: No. Required: new account form details, CIP identity verification, OFAC screening, principal approval.

front: JTWROS vs tenants in common at death
back: JTWROS: decedent's interest passes to the survivor(s), bypassing probate. TIC: decedent's share goes to their ESTATE.
Rep choosing the Asset, Action, or Amount = discretion → WRITTEN authorization first. Time/price only = not discretion (good that day).

front: Reg T initial margin & industry maintenance minimums
back: Initial 50% (Reg T). Maintenance: 25% equity long, 30% short. FINRA minimum to open: $2,000.
Required: credit agreement + hypothecation agreement. OPTIONAL: loan consent form (lets the firm lend your shares to shorts).

front: Accounts that can NEVER be margin accounts
back: Retirement accounts (IRAs) and UGMA/UTMA custodial accounts. New issues: not marginable for 30 days.
Disclosure, Care, Conflict of interest, Compliance — plus Form CRS to every retail customer.
Reasonable-basis (suitable for anyone?), customer-specific (for THIS customer?), quantitative (is the trading pattern excessive?).
Irrevocable gift; one custodian, one minor; minor's SSN; no margin/shorts; assets transfer outright at majority.
529: DONOR keeps control (can even reclaim, paying tax + penalty on earnings). UGMA: irrevocable — the CHILD takes control at majority.
Traditional: deductible in, ordinary income out, RMDs at 73. Roth: after-tax in, TAX-FREE out (59½ + 5-year rule), NO lifetime RMDs.

front: IRA early-withdrawal penalty and key exceptions
back: 10% before 59½ (plus tax). Exceptions: death, disability, first home ($10k), certain medical/education costs.
Trustee-to-trustee transfer: unlimited, untaxed. Rollover: 60 days to redeposit, ONE IRA-to-IRA rollover per 12 months.

front: Worst investment to put inside an IRA
back: Municipal bonds — tax-free yield wasted in a tax-deferred account (withdrawals become taxable anyway).

front: Who bears investment risk in a defined benefit plan?
back: The EMPLOYER — the payout is promised. In a 401(k)/defined contribution plan, the employee bears it.

front: Maximum civil penalty for insider trading
back: Treble damages — 3× the profit gained OR LOSS AVOIDED — plus disgorgement. Criminal: up to 20 years.

front: Is a tipper liable if they never traded?
back: Yes — passing material nonpublic information is a violation; the tippee who trades is liable too.
Trading ahead of a known customer/block order (or pending research) to profit from the move it will cause. Prohibited.
The PATTERN — turnover and cost-to-equity vs objectives — not individual trades. 'Customer didn't complain' is no defense.

front: Selling away vs outside business activity
back: OBA (non-securities job): prior written NOTICE. Private securities transactions for compensation: written APPROVAL required.
$100 per person per year tied to business. Business entertainment (rep attends) is separate but must be reasonable.
CASH over $10,000 in one day (aggregated) → file with FinCEN within 15 days. Structuring deposits to dodge it is itself a crime.
Suspicious activity of $5,000+ → file within 30 days — and NEVER tell the customer.
Placement (cash enters — most detectable) → Layering (transfers bury the trail) → Integration (funds look clean).

front: Guaranteeing a customer against loss is…
back: ALWAYS prohibited. Sharing in an account is allowed only with firm + customer written approval, proportionate to contribution (family exception).
Paying for a purchase with proceeds of its own sale in a cash account → 90-day freeze (cash up front).
1933: PRIMARY market — registration and prospectuses. 1934: SECONDARY market — created the SEC; antifraud (10b-5), margin, proxies, insider reports.
Mutual funds, closed-end funds, and UITs. (Advisers Act of 1940: investment advisers. Trust Indenture Act of 1939: corporate bond indentures.)

front: How long is a passed SIE valid?
back: 4 years. Open to anyone 18+, no sponsorship. Representative registration requires a firm-sponsored top-off (Series 6 or 7).

front: Registration lapses how long after leaving the industry?
back: 2 years after the U5 (MQP with CE can extend to 5). U4 and U5 filings are due within 30 days.
ANNUALLY by December 31 for every registered person. Miss it → registration inactive (no commissions). Firm element: annual, firm-designed.
Within 10 years: ANY felony, or a money/securities-related MISDEMEANOR (theft, forgery, bribery). Plus regulator bars/expulsions.
More than 25 retail investors in 30 days = retail communication → principal PRE-approval. 25 or fewer = correspondence → review procedures only.
8 a.m.–9 p.m. in the CUSTOMER'S time zone; honor firm and national do-not-call lists. Exceptions: existing customers, prior express invitation.
Privacy notice at opening and annually + the right to OPT OUT of sharing nonpublic personal info with unaffiliated third parties.
FINRA's free public database of firm/rep registration and disciplinary history, drawn from the CRD.
Arbitration if the customer agreed in writing (standard account agreements do). Awards are final and binding — no appeal. Class actions: never arbitration.
3 years, first 2 easily accessible. Social media: static profiles = retail communication; live interactive posts = correspondence.
Annual report, quarterly report, and material-event report — 1934 Act filings for public companies.
No — a nonprofit, industry-funded corporation (and not a regulator). FDIC IS a government corporation.

front: Where do most corporate and municipal BONDS trade?
back: Over the counter through dealer networks — not on exchanges.

front: Open orders on the ex-dividend date
back: Orders BELOW the market (buy limits, sell stops) are reduced by the dividend unless marked DNR.

front: Options strategy for income on stock you own
back: Write covered calls. Neutral-to-bullish income without stock: write puts (you'd buy the stock at the strike if assigned).

front: Ratings measure which risk — and which NOT?
back: DEFAULT (credit) risk only. They say nothing about interest-rate or market risk.
Firms must try to obtain a trusted contact; they may place temporary holds on disbursements when exploitation of a specified adult (65+/impaired) is suspected.
